column5: The protein encoded by this gene belongs to the major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class II beta chain paralogs. Class II molecules are heterodimers consisting of an alpha (DRA) and a beta chain (DQB), both anchored in the membrane. They play a central role in the immune system by presenting peptides derived from extracellular proteins. Class II molecules are expressed in antigen presenting cells (APC: B lymphocytes, macrophages, dendritic cells). The beta chain is approximately 26-28 kDa and its length is defined by the exon borders. Genetic variation in these genes is associated with susceptibility to a number of autoimmune diseases, such as diabetes mellitus type 1, multiple sclerosis, rheumatoid arthritis and celiac disease.

column24: \Acheiropody is primarily characterized by the absence of hands and feet. Other symptoms may include shortened or absent long bones in the arms and legs@ and sometimes other skeletal abnormalities.\""
column25: \Acheiropody is caused by mutations in the LMBR1 gene@ which plays a crucial role in limb development. The mutations disrupt the normal signaling pathways required for the formation of limbs during embryogenesis.\""
column31: \Supportive care@ physical therapy@ and occupational therapy are the mainstays of treatment. Prosthetics may be used to improve mobility and function.\""

column37: \Genetic mutations are the primary risk factor for acheiropody. There are no known environmental or lifestyle factors that increase the risk of developing this condition.\""
column38: \Acheiropody is a congenital condition@ meaning it is present at birth. The absence of hands and feet is evident immediately after birth.\""
column39: \Acheiropody is caused by mutations in the LMBR1 gene@ which is involved in limb development.\""
column40: \Acheiropody is an autosomal recessive condition@ meaning that both parents must carry a copy of the mutated gene for a child to be affected. Family history of the condition increases the risk.\""
column41: \The severity of acheiropody varies depending on the extent of limb malformation. In some cases@ there may be complete absence of hands and feet@ while in others@ there may be residual limb structures.\""
column42: \Diagnosis is typically made based on clinical examination and imaging studies such as X-rays. Genetic testing can confirm the diagnosis by identifying mutations in the LMBR1 gene.\""
column43: \Complications may include difficulty with mobility@ self-care@ and social interaction. Psychological support and counseling may be beneficial for affected individuals and their families.\""
column44: \Acheiropody is a very rare condition with an estimated prevalence of less than 1 in 1 million births. It is more common in certain populations due to founder effects.\""
column45: \The prognosis for individuals with acheiropody depends on the severity of the condition and the availability of supportive care. With appropriate management@ individuals with acheiropody can lead fulfilling lives.\"
